title: User interface control of responsive devices
abstract: Among other things, a user interface device has a sensor configured to detect, at a wrist of a human, nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with an intended contraction of a muscle to cause a rapid motion of a finger. An output provides information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with the intended contraction of the muscle to an interpreter of the information.
claims:
Claims Claims 1. An apparatus comprising a wrist worn user interface device having a sensor configured to detect, at a top surface of a wrist of a human, nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with intended contractions and extensions of a muscle to cause a gesture comprising repeated flicks of only an index finger of the hand attached to the wrist, each of the repeated flicks of the gesture having a duration less than one-half second and comprising a contraction and an extension of only the index finger, an output configured to provide information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with the intended contractions and extensions of the muscle, and an interpreter configured to receive and interpret the information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and provide an indication of the intended contractions and extensions of the muscle to cause the gesture comprising repeated flicks of only the index finger, the indication being provided to a responsive device not located on the hand attached to the wrist to cause the responsive device to treat the indication as a gesture invoking a user interface control of a display. 2. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a responsive device coupled to the interpreter and configured to respond to the intended contractions and extensions of the muscle by an action. 3. The apparatus of claim 2 in which the responsive device is configured to respond by an action that includes changing an audio or visual presentation to the human. 4. The apparatus of claim 3 in which the responsive device is configured to respond by an action that includes changing the audio or visual presentation as if the intended contraction of the muscle corresponded to an invocation of a user interface control. 5. An apparatus comprising a wrist-wom user interface device having a sensor configured to detect, at a top surface of a wrist of a human hand, nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated (a) with an activation of a muscle intended to cause a first motion of the hand, and (b) with an activation of a muscle intended to cause, at a time during or at the end of the first motion, a stationary hold of the hand , 

 an output configured to provide to an interpreter information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with the activations intended to cause the first motion and the stationary hold , and an interpreter configured to receive and interpret the information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and to provide (a) a first indication of the activation intended to cause the first motion, and (b) a second indication of the activation of the motion intended to cause the stationary hold , the first indication configured to cause an action to be performed on a display of a responsive device not mounted on the hand, the second indication being configured to cause the responsive device to continue the performance of the action to be performed on the display of the responsive device during at least a part of the stationary hold . 6. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the action includes changing an audio or visual presentation to the human. 7. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the motion of the hand comprises a motion of a finger of the hand. 8. The apparatus of claim 6 in which the action includes continuing the change of the audio or visual presentation . 9. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the first motion comprises a rotation of the hand , and the second motion comprises the hand being held stationary during or at the end of the rotation. 10. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the first motion comprises an index finger lift, and the second motion comprises the finger being held stationary during or at the end of the rotation. 11. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the action to be performed on the display of the responsive device comprises motion of a displayed graphical element in response to the first motion of the hand and to the stationary hold. 12. The apparatus of claim 5 in which the first motion of the hand comprises rotation of a palm relative to an elbow. 13. The apparatus of claim 12 in which the action to be performed comprises rotating a displayed graphical element in response to the rotation of the palm relative to the elbow. 14. The apparatus of claim 11 in which the displayed graphical element comprises a three-dimensional representation of an object and the action to be performed comprises rotating the displayed graphical element in three-dimensional space 15. The apparatus of claim 11 in which the displayed graphical element comprises a digital camera control and the action to be performed comprises adjusting the digital camera control. 16. The apparatus of claim 11 in which the displayed graphical element comprises characters of an access code and the action to be performed comprises rotating the displayed graphical element to select characters of the access code. 17. An apparatus comprising two or more user interface devices configured to generate outputs, at least two of the two or more user interface devices having at least two electroneurography sensors configured to detect, at a dorsal side of a wrist of the human, nerve signals associated with intended contractions of two or more muscles and to generate corresponding sensor outputs, a processor configured to derive information corresponding to and representing nerve signals from the sensor outputs of the electroneurography sensors, at least one of the user interface devices comprising an inertial measurement unit (IMU), at least one of the user interface devices comprising a wrist-wom user interface device, two or more responsive devices, and an interpreter configured (i) to generate interpreted outputs based on (a) the information derived by the processor corresponding to and representing nerve signals from the sensor outputs of the electroneurography sensors , (b) ajoint consideration of the 

 information derived by the processor corresponding to and representing nerve signals from the sensor outputs of the two electroneurography sensors of each of the user interface devices, and (c) a separate consideration of the information derived by the processor corresponding to and representing nerve signals from the sensor outputs of each of the two electroneurography sensors of each of the user interface devices, and (ii) to provide the interpreted outputs to a controller configured to effect actions by the two or more responsive devices. 18. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the inertial measurement unit is calibrated using a calibration technique. 19. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the inertial measurement unit is configured to access a value corresponding to a reference point. 20. The apparatus of claim 19 in which the reference point is independent of the position of the inertial measurement unit. 21. The apparatus of claim 19 in which a vector provided by the inertial measurement unit is compared to a second vector that is calculated using the value corresponding to the reference point. 22. The apparatus of claim 18 in which the calibration technique is performed after a pre defined number of spatial calculations of the inertial measurement unit. 23. The apparatus of claim 18 in which the calibration technique is performed in response to a command from the human. 24. The apparatus of claim 17 in which at least two of the user interfaces devices are configured to be worn by different humans and comprise wrist-wom devices. 25. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the interpreter is configured to provide the interpreted outputs to a controller configured to effect the actions by altering presentations by the responsive devices . 26. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the interpreter is configured to provide the interpreted outputs to a controller configured to cause at least one of the responsive devices to perform a zoom. 27. The apparatus of claim 26 in which the zoom alters a presentation comprising a display. 28. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the interpreter is configured to provide the output to a controller configured to cause the responsive device to capture an image, video, or a sound, or a combination of them. 29. The apparatus of claim 17 in which at least one of the responsive devices is configured to operate compatibly with a peripheral device that can generate an output for controlling the action of the responsive device. 30. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the action of the responsive device is controlled without the human touching the responsive device. 31. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the interpreter is configured to provide the interpreted outputs to the responsive devices based on a Bluetooth round robin. 32. The apparatus of claim 17 comprising an intermediary routing device that manages connections with the responsive devices . 33. The apparatus of claim 32 in which the interpreter is configured to send the interpreted outputs to the responsive devices through the intermediary routing device. 34. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the responsive device is configured to provide a presentation to a user of at least one of the user interface device. 35. The apparatus of claim 17 in which the sensor of at least one of the user interface devices is configured to detect additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals of a human and generate data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals, and the interpreter is configured to make interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 36. The apparatus of claim 35 in which the interpreter is configured to generate the output based on a joint consideration of the interpretations of the data representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and the interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 37. The apparatus of claim 35 in which the interpreter is configured to generate the output based on a separate consideration of the interpretations of the data representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and the interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 38. An apparatus comprising 

 a user interface device having a sensor configured to detect, at a top of a wrist of a human, biometric nerve or other tissue electrical signals indicative of a unique identity of the human, and an output configured to provide biometric signal data representative of the signals to an interpreter of the information for use in determining the unique identity of the human. 39. The apparatus of claim 38 comprising an authentication system for generating a verification output with respect to an identity of the human based on the provided information. 40. The apparatus of claim 38 in which the authentication system is configured to control the humanâs access to a responsive device based on the verification output. 41. The apparatus of claim 38 in which the verification output is based on the biometric signal data. 42. The apparatus of claim 41 in which the biometric signal data is indicative of wrist acceleration, wrist orientation, or other motion or position of the wrist of the human. 43. The apparatus of claim 41 in which the biometric signal data is provided during a passive mode of the user interface device. 44. The apparatus of claim 41 in which the biometric signal data is provided during an active mode of a responsive device being controlled by the human through the user interface device. 45. The apparatus of claim 39 in which the verification output is generated repeatedly at successive times. 46. The apparatus of claim 39 in which the verification output is generated continuously. 47. An apparatus comprising a user interface device having a sensor configured to detect, at a top of a wrist of a human, nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with an intended contraction of a muscle to cause a motion of a part of a human, and 

 an output to provide information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with the intended contraction of the muscle to a controller associated with a responsive device. 48. The apparatus of claim 47 comprising the controller. 49. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the controller is at least partly in the user interface device. 50. The apparatus of claim 59 in which the controller is configured to cause the responsive device to perform a selection. 62. The apparatus of claim 62 in which the zoom alters a presentation comprising a display. 64. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the controller is configured to cause the responsive device to capture an image, video, or a sound, or a combination of them. 65. The apparatus of claim 61 in which the selection is performed on alphanumerical characters. 66. The apparatus of claim 59 in which the responsive device is configured to operate compatibly with a peripheral device that can generate an output for controlling the action of the responsive device. 67. The apparatus of claim 66 in which the controller is configured to cause the responsive device to perform an action that can also be caused by the output generated by the peripheral device. 68. The apparatus of claim 67 in which the controller is configured not to control the action of the responsive device unless the human is in contact with the peripheral device. 69. The apparatus of claim 66 in which the peripheral device comprises a handheld peripheral device. 70. The apparatus of claim 51 in which the action of the responsive device is controlled without the human touching the responsive device. 71. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the controller is configured to send the output to the responsive device based on a Bluetooth round robin. 72. The apparatus of claim 47 comprising an intermediary routing device that manages connections with the responsive device. 73. The apparatus of claim 72 in which the controller is configured to send the output to the responsive device through the intermediary routing device. 74. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the responsive device is configured to provide a presentation to a user of the user interface device. 75. The apparatus of claim 74 in which the presentation comprises a display or sound or both. 76. The apparatus of claim 47 comprising a second user interface device configured to detect additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals of a human and generate data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals, and one or more interpreters are configured to make interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 77. The apparatus of claim 76 in which the controller is configured to generate the output based on a joint consideration of the interpretations of the data representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and the interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 78. The apparatus of claim 76 in which the controller is configured to generate the output based on a separate consideration of the interpretations of the data representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals and the interpretations of the data representative of the additional nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 79. The apparatus of claim 80 in which the time delay corresponds to a time required for interpretation of the data representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals. 82. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the nerve or other tissue electrical signals of the human detected by the sensor correspond to one or more gestures or intended gestures. 83. The apparatus of claim 47 in which the nerve or other tissue electrical signals of the human detected by the sensor correspond to one or more motions or intended motions of the human. 84. The apparatus of claim 83 in which the one or more interpreters are configured to provide one or more interpretations indicative of the one or more motions or intended motions of the human. 85. The apparatus of claim 46 in which the one or more motions or intended motions of the human comprise one or more muscle contractions or extensions in an upper extremity of the human. 86. An apparatus comprising a user interface device having a sensor configured to detect nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with an intended contraction of a muscle to cause a motion of a part of a human and an output coupled to a distribution system configured to distribute information representative of the nerve or other tissue electrical signals associated with the intended contraction of the muscle to two or more responsive devices not located on the human , the output configured to cause , in combination with one or more additional outputs associated with other user interface devices, actions corresponding to the intended contraction of the muscle, the actions being performed by the two or more responsive devices in parallel in response to the output of the user interface device and the outputs of the other user interface devices. 87. The apparatus of claim 86 comprising a feedback component configured to provide feedback to the human indicative of the action by at least one of the responsive devices and to guide the human to control the intended contraction of the muscle for further action of the two or more responsive devices . 88. The apparatus of claim 87 in which the feedback component comprises a haptic element configured to provide haptic feedback to the human. 89. The apparatus of claim 87 in which the feedback component is configured to receive feedback information from one or more of the responsive devices . 90. The apparatus of claim 87 comprising at least one of the responsive devices and in which the responsive device includes the feedback component. 91. The apparatus of claim 90 in which the responsive device comprises smart glasses. 92. The apparatus of claim 91 in which the feedback comprises a visible element displayed by the smart glasses. 93. The apparatus of claim 87 in which the feedback comprises an element displayed by at least one of the responsive devices . 94. The apparatus of claim 87 in which the feedback comprises a sound produced by one or more of the responsive devices . 95. the apparatus of claim 86 in which the distributed information comprises raw data representing the nerve or other tissue electrical signals, and the output provides the distributed information to an interpreter external to the user interface device and configured to interpret the raw data to cause the actions by at least one of the responsive devices. 96. The apparatus of claim 95 in which the output is coupled to the responsive device. 97. The apparatus of claim 10 in which the output is coupled to a communication channel that is also coupled to the responsive device. 98. The apparatus of claim 97 in which the communication channel comprises a mesh network. 99. The apparatus of claim 97 in which the output is coupled to an intermediary device that is also coupled to the responsive device. 100. The apparatus of claim 97 in which the output is coupled to a socket. 101. The apparatus of claim 97 in which the output is coupled to the distribution system to distribute the output to responsive devices located at distances from the user interface device greater than can be reached by short-range wireless communications. 102. The apparatus of claim 86 in which the output is coupled to the distribution system through a socket. 103. The apparatus of claim 86 in which the output is coupled to a mesh network. 104. The apparatus of claim 103 in which each of the two or more responsive devices comprises a node in the mesh network. 105. The apparatus of claim 104 in which at least one of the two or more responsive devices is configured to distribute the output to another responsive device. 106. The apparatus of claim 104 in which at least one of the two or more responsive devices is configured to issue a command to another responsive device in response to the output of the user interface device. 107. The apparatus of claim 86 in which each of the one or more additional outputs is associated with a different user interface device. 108. the apparatus of claim 86 in which at least two of the user interface devices comprise wrist-worn devices. 109. A method comprising receiving first signals representing a manipulation of a physical feature of a physical device by one or more fingers of a hand of a user, receiving second signals representing tissue electrical activity indicative of and occurring prior to the manipulation, processing the first signals and the second signals to identify the occurrence of the manipulation, and sending a control signal to a game or other application with which the user is interacting, the control signal corresponding to the identified occurrence of the manipulation. 110. The method of claim 109 in which the manipulation of the physical feature and the tissue electrical activity indicative of the manipulation occur at different times. 111. The method of claim 110 in which the processing of the first signals and the second signals comprises determining which of the manipulation of the physical feature and the tissue electrical activity more accurately represents the occurrence of the manipulation. 112. The method of claim 110 in which the processing of the first signals and the second signals comprises determining that the earlier of the manipulation of the physical feature in the tissue electrical activity more accurately represents the occurrence of the manipulation. 113. The method of claim 109 in which sending the control signal to a game or other application comprises posting an event to an event queue of an operating system associated with the game or the other application. 114. The method of claim 109 in which the processing of the first signals and the second signals comprises applying the first signals and the second signals to a classifier. 115. The method of claim 114 comprising training the classifier based on first signals representing manipulations of the physical feature and second signals representing tissue electrical activities. 116. The method of claim 115 in which training the classifier comprises training the classifier repeatedly while the user is interacting with the game or other application. 117. The method of claim 115 in which training the classifier comprises applying a dedicated calibration routine before the user begins to interact with the game or other application. 118. The method of claim 115 comprising receiving instructions from the user about the training of the classifier. 119. The method of claim 109 in which the processing of the first signals and the second signals comprises applying the first signals and the second signals to a selected classifier among a set of classifiers. 120. The method of claim 109 in which the selected classifier is selected based on input of the user. 121. The method of claim 109 in which the tissue electrical activity corresponds to a contraction or extension or both of a muscle of the user. 122. The method of claim 109 in which the physical device comprises a physical mouse and the physical feature comprises a button or switch of the mouse. 123. The method of claim 109 in which the manipulation comprises a mouse click. 124. The method of claim 109 in which the tissue electrical activity occurs at the anterior side of the wrist of the user. 125. The method of claim 109 comprising receiving a signal indicative of a state of physical contact between the user and the physical device, and the processing of the first signals and the second signals to identify the occurrence of the manipulation includes taking account of the signal indicative of the state of physical contact. 126. The method of claim 109 in which the first signals are received as a stream of samples. 127. The method of claim 109 in which the second signals are received as a stream of samples. 128. The method of claim 109 comprising time stamping the first signals or the second signals or both. 129. The method of claim 109 in which two or more channels of the second signals are received. 130. The method of claim 109 comprising receiving third signals from sensors facing the posterior wrist on the radial side of the user. 131. The method of claim 109 in which the second signals are received from sensors belonging to a tattoo-based or sticker-based component. 132. The method of claim 109 in which the second signals are received from a user interface device. 133. The method of claim 109 in which the second signals are received from implanted sensors. 134. The method of claim 109 in which the second signals are received from sensors on a watch. 135. The method of claim 109 in which the physical device and the user interface device are electrically coupled. 136. The method of claim 109 in which the physical device and the user interface device are mechanically coupled. 137. The method of claim 114 in which the classifier is customized for a context in which the game or other application is used. 138. The method of claim 138 in which the context comprises the model or particular unit of the model of the physical device. 139. The method of claim 138 in which the context comprises the identity of the user. 140. The method of claim 138 in which the context comprises the behavior of the user interface device. 141. The method of claim 138 in which the context comprises style of manipulation by the user. 142. The method of claim 109 comprising measuring the effectiveness of the use of the user interface device by the user. 143. The method of claim 109 comprising reporting to the user information about the first signals, the second signals, the physical device, the processing of the first signals and the second signals, or the sending of the control signal to the game or other application, or combinations of two or more of those. 144. The method of claim 109 comprising receiving signal representing a position or orientation of the hand of the user relative to the physical device. 145. The method of claim 109 comprising determining characteristics of the manipulation based on the first signals or the second signals or both. 146. The method of claim 145 in which the characteristics comprise the forcefulness of the manipulation. 147. The method of claim 145 in which the characteristics comprise identities of one or more fingers involved in the manipulation. 148. The method of claim 45 in which the characteristics comprise wrist rotations. 149. The method of claim 109 comprising receiving third signals from an IMU and in which the processing takes account of the third signals from the IMU.
